title: American pot roast
subtitle: Slow-braised beef chuck with carrots, potatoes, and gravy
excerpt: A whole piece of beef chuck braised for three hours with onion, carrots, potatoes, and stock until it becomes fork-tender and the braising liquid reduces to a rich, beefy gravy. One pot, one oven temperature, no technique more complex than patient waiting.
sourceNotes: Pot roast is documented in American domestic cookery from the nineteenth century. Fannie Farmer's 'The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book' (1896, Project Gutenberg #7544) includes pot-roasted beef preparations. The dish became a mid-twentieth-century American Sunday dinner staple, associated with the slow oven braises that could cook unattended while the household went about its day.

content.text: Chuck is the right cut for this: it has enough connective tissue to turn from tough to silky over the long braise, and enough fat to keep the meat moist through the full three hours. A leaner cut will dry out and become stringy. The meat is browned hard before the braising liquid goes in, do not skip this step. The deep crust that forms in the first 5 minutes is what gives the final gravy its colour and depth.

The pot goes into the oven at 160°C and stays there, covered, for 3 hours. No checking, no basting, no adjusting. At the three-hour mark, the meat should pull apart with a fork with almost no resistance. If it does not, give it another 30 minutes, the braising process cannot be rushed and some pieces of chuck take longer than others.

items.name: Beef chuck
items.prepNote: whole piece, tied with string if necessary

content.text: Heat the oven to 160°C fan. Pat the {{beef-chuck}} of beef chuck dry with kitchen paper and season generously with salt and pepper on all sides. Heat a thin layer of oil in the casserole over high heat until smoking. Sear the beef for 3 to 4 minutes per side without moving until a deep brown crust forms all over. Remove and set aside.

Add the {{onion}} and {{garlic}} to the casserole and cook over medium heat for 3 minutes. Pour in the {{stock-beef}} of beef stock, {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes, and {{worcestershire-sauce}} of Worcestershire sauce, scraping up the browned bits from the base. Return the beef to the pot. Nestle the {{carrot}} and {{rosemary}} and {{thyme}} around the meat. Bring to a simmer, then cover and transfer to the oven.

After 2 hours, add the {{potato}} halves to the pot, pushing them into the braising liquid. Return to the oven, covered, for a further hour. At the 3-hour mark, check that the beef pulls apart with a fork. If not, return for another 30 minutes. Rest, covered, for 15 minutes before serving. Remove the herb sprigs. Serve the beef in chunks or pulled, with the vegetables and braising juices spooned over.

items.fix: Return to the oven for another 30 to 45 minutes; the transition from tough to tender can happen quickly, check every 20 minutes once past 3 hours
items.cause: Tough pieces of chuck vary; some take longer
items.symptom: Beef is still tough after 3 hours

items.fix: Remove the beef and vegetables, then simmer the braising liquid uncovered on the hob for 10 minutes to reduce and concentrate
items.cause: Too much liquid, or lid too loose allowing steam to escape

items.fix: Add potatoes no more than 1 hour before the end; keep pieces large (halved, not quartered)
items.cause: Added too early or too small a cut

items.fix: Pat the meat very dry before searing and ensure the pan is smoking hot; moisture in the pan prevents browning
items.cause: Meat too wet, or pan not hot enough
items.symptom: Surface of the beef did not brown when searing

content.text: Pot roast is the American take on the European braise. The French daube and the Italian brasato work the same way. Cook a tough, cheap cut in liquid until it falls apart. By the middle of the last century it had become the classic American Sunday dinner. The Midwest and Northeast claimed it most. A cold Sunday and a heavy lidded pot on the stove felt right together. The dish is one of the few that rewards no skill at all. The longer it cooks and the longer it rests, the better it tastes. Reheated the next day, it is better still.
